name: TOOM
classification_notes: All-caps horror comic font inspired by the Leroy Lettering System used by EC Comics in the 1950s. Captures the mechanically consistent hand-lettering quality of mid-century horror comics. Name blends TOON and TOMB.
summary: A 1950s horror comic lettering font modelled on the Leroy lettering device used by EC Comics, with skulls, excitement lines and deliberately misprinted characters.
description: TOOM replicates the eerie mechanical consistency of the Leroy Lettering System, the technical drawing device that gave EC Comics their distinctive all-caps style. Christopher Sperandio's design includes period-specific extras: skulls, excitement lines, and intentionally misprinted glyphs that heighten the vintage horror aesthetic. Three all-caps variants cover regular, italic and bold italic settings.
foundry_blurb: TOOM captures the look of handwriting but with a lifeless consistency — the Leroy Lettering System look recreated for modern designers.

name: Dragster Display Fonts
classification_notes: Collection of 8 display fonts inspired by classic old-school race car lettering. Includes 2 alphabets and 6 numeral fonts, with stencil and italic variants across 17 variations total.
summary: Eight vintage racing-inspired display fonts drawing from mid-century race car lettering, helmets and signage traditions.
description: Dragster is a vintage racing typeface collection by illustrator Travis Price, comprising 8 fonts across 17 variations including stencil and italic cuts. The designs draw directly from classic race logo lettering, helmet graphics and trackside signage from the mid-century era. Dramatic and highly legible, the collection works well across posters, vehicle graphics and band promotion.
foundry_blurb: Dragster draws from classic old-school race car lettering used on race logos, car numbers, racing numbers, helmets and signage.

name: Leutner
classification_notes: Four-layer geometric display typeface with Main, Fill, Base and 3D layers for dimensional effects. Inspired by late 20th-century modernist geometry. Designed by Aaron Sechrist of okPANTS, Cleveland.
summary: A layered geometric display typeface with four coordinating files that stack to create dimensional headline effects.
description: Leutner is a bold geometric display face built as a four-layer system — Main, Fill, Base and 3D — designed to be stacked for dimensional typographic effects. Aaron Sechrist of okPANTS drew from late 20th-century modernist geometry while developing a typeface with its own distinct personality rather than a period pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Eyeball-grabbing, hypnotic and sturdy — Leutner's layered system lets you create eye-catching typography quickly.

name: Blackbike
classification_notes: Display script inspired by rugged vintage motorbikes. Two-font family with clean and rough/distressed variants. 279 glyphs with 32 alternates and ligatures. Designed by Simon Walker of Beasts of England.
summary: A display script with vintage motorcycle character, offered in clean and distressed variants with 279 glyphs and 32 alternates.
description: Blackbike draws its visual language from the aesthetic of rugged classic motorbikes: the polished-versus-patina duality of vintage machinery. Simon Walker of Beasts of England offers both a clean and a rough variant, giving designers the choice between refined and weathered. The 279-glyph set includes 32 alternates and ligatures for flexible typesetting.
foundry_blurb: A unique and dynamic script drawn from the character of rugged vintage motorbikes, with clean and rough variants for maximum flexibility.

name: New Jack
classification_notes: Modern take on Fraktur-inflected blackletter, specifically designed for legibility. Some reviewers classify it closer to Fraktur than classical blackletter. Available in OTF and TTF.
summary: A contemporary blackletter with Fraktur character, prioritising legibility over traditional ornate complexity.
description: New Jack brings a mid-century German design sensibility to blackletter without sacrificing readability. Travis Price has pared back the ornate complexity typical of the category, producing a face that reads clearly at poster scale. Users note it tends toward Fraktur rather than classical blackletter, making it versatile for branding, apparel and retro poster work.
foundry_blurb: New Jack is a bold, easily legible blackletter designed for logos, branding, apparel and poster applications.

name: DERAILED!
classification_notes: All-caps display font drawing spiritual inspiration from Fumetti neri — Italian horror comics of the 1960s–70s. Bold, stark letterforms for headline use. Single weight.
summary: An all-caps display font channelling the stark visual language of Italian Fumetti neri horror comics from the 1960s–70s.
description: DERAILED! takes its spiritual cues from the sardonic darkness of Italian Fumetti neri — the horror comics known for their bleak, edgy tone. Christopher Sperandio has translated that energy into a stark all-caps display face suited to small doses: comic book lettering, horror zines and vintage graphic design work.
foundry_blurb: DERAILED! captures the spiritual essence of dark Italian comic titles — stark, bold and best used in small amounts for maximum impact.

name: Thermo-Nuke
classification_notes: All-caps bold display font with handmade, imperfect quality. Horror comic aesthetic. Single weight. Designed by Christopher Sperandio.
summary: A bold all-caps horror comic display face with deliberately imperfect letterforms for use in comics, zines and retro projects.
description: Thermo-Nuke brings an intentionally handmade quality to its bold all-caps letterforms, replicating the irregular feel of hand-drawn horror comic titles. Christopher Sperandio, the designer behind TOOM, leans into imperfection here — the irregular strokes pair well with comics, zines and any project that needs visible analogue character.
foundry_blurb: The handmade feel pairs nicely with comics, zines, and other projects that need imperfection and a sense of existential dread.

name: Bandstand
classification_notes: Bold display typeface inspired by 1950s hand-drawn poster lettering and rock and roll culture. Available in OTF and TTF.
summary: A high-energy display face drawn from 1950s rock and roll poster lettering, combining mid-century swagger with strong legibility.
description: Bandstand captures the electricity of mid-century jukebox nights in a bold, legible display typeface. Travis Price drew from hand-drawn poster lettering of the 1950s music scene, producing a face that works equally well for band promotions, retro branding and vintage-inspired packaging.
foundry_blurb: Bandstand draws inspiration from hand-drawn poster lettering from the 1950s and rock and roll-inspired letterforms with hand-crafted details.

name: Hey!
classification_notes: Layered mid-century display font with five coordinating styles (Regular, Inlay, Inline, and clean/dotted variants), over 60 supplementary vector elements, and full Western/Central/Southeastern European language support. Designed by Lenny Terenzi.
summary: A layered mid-century display typeface evoking lost jazz lounge posters, with five coordinating styles and over 60 vector extras.
description: Hey! is a five-style layered display system by Lenny Terenzi, evoking the visual world of bebop posters and 1950s diner signage. The set combines Regular, Inlay and Inline cuts with clean and dotted variants for dimensional layering effects. Extensive alternate characters, discretionary ligatures and over 60 supplementary vector elements round out the package.
foundry_blurb: Hey! feels like something you'd dig out of a thrift bin between a Slinky and a stack of Archie comics — a lost jazz lounge poster reborn as a typeface.

name: Nitelife
classification_notes: Six-style mid-century display typeface inspired by vintage neon signage and jazz culture. Staggered baseline alternates and mid-century ornaments. Designed by Lenny Terenzi.
summary: A six-style mid-century display family channelling blues clubs, cocktail menus and neon signage, with staggered baseline alternates.
description: Nitelife pulls its inspiration from the visual language of mid-century jazz and nightlife — neon bar signs, cocktail menus and album sleeve typography. Lenny Terenzi built the family across six styles including Grime and Boogie variants, with alternates for the classic staggered baseline effect central to vintage lounge aesthetics.
foundry_blurb: Nitelife pulls from the world of blues clubs, cocktail menus, jazz sleeves and mid-century nightlife culture — complete with that classic mid-century bounce.

name: Right Price Font
classification_notes: Three-style casual display script (Regular, Bold, Inline) inspired by vintage concert posters and hand-painted display cards. Designed by Travis Price.
summary: A three-style casual script nodding to old concert posters and hand-painted vintage display cards.
description: Right Price draws directly from the hand-lettered tradition of concert posters and vintage retail signage. Travis Price delivers three coordinating styles — Regular, Bold and Inline — for dimensional layering or standalone use. The face works across posters, signage, t-shirt design and vintage-themed branding.
foundry_blurb: Right Price is a nod to old concert posters and hand painted display cards — a casual script with mid-century swagger.

name: Cereal Killa
classification_notes: Four-alphabet display family inspired by vintage cereal box packaging design from the 1950s–60s. Designed by Travis Price.
summary: Four coordinating display alphabets evoking the kitschy visual language of 1950s–60s cereal box packaging.
description: Cereal Killa channels the bold, legible aesthetic of mid-century commercial packaging — specifically the grocer's special energy of vintage cereal boxes. Travis Price has produced four alphabets that work together or independently, each carrying a dramatic but readable character well suited to posters, comics and retro branding.
foundry_blurb: Cereal Killa has perfect balance and volume across four variations, drawing from the kitschy retro world of vintage cereal box packaging.

name: Hotel Motel
classification_notes: Casual sentence-case script inspired by hand-painted motel signs of 1950s America. Alternate glyphs and ligatures. OTF and TTF formats.
summary: A casual script modelled on hand-painted motel signage from 1950s America, with alternates and ligatures for flexible setting.
description: Hotel Motel translates the laid-back imprecision of roadside motel signs into a digital script that retains hand-painted warmth. Travis Price balances looseness with designer precision, delivering alternates and ligatures that allow for varied composition without awkward breaks common in other scripts.
foundry_blurb: A casual sentence-case script inspired by hand-painted motel signs of 1950s America — that perfect blend of laid-back looseness and designer precision.

name: Sure Shot Font
classification_notes: Three-style layered brush script (Brush, Inline, Inlay) inspired by vintage hand-painted retail signage. Slightly condensed proportions. Extended Latin character set.
summary: A confident three-style brush script drawn from vintage retail signage, with Brush, Inline and Inlay cuts for dimensional layering.
description: Sure Shot is a bold, slightly condensed brush script that takes its proportions and energy from vintage hand-painted retail displays. The three coordinating styles — Brush, Inline and Inlay — combine for dimensional lettering treatments or work individually at headline scale.
foundry_blurb: Sure Shot features the confident strokes of a professional lettering artist, condensed just enough for striking headlines and layered dimensional treatments.

name: Palm Canyon Drive
classification_notes: Monoline script inspired by retro matchbook covers, travel postcards, Tiki bars and Hollywood. Standard alpha-numeric characters and popular catchwords. Designed by Amy Hood of Hoodzpah Design Co.
summary: A classy monoline script evoking retro matchbooks, travel postcards and mid-century Hollywood with alternates and popular catchwords.
description: Palm Canyon Drive captures the classy yet unpretentious tone of mid-century California — matchbook lettering, Tiki bar signage and vintage Hollywood graphic work. Amy Hood of Hoodzpah Design delivers an extensive glyph collection with alternates and popular catchwords, avoiding the awkward ligature breaks common in lesser scripts.
foundry_blurb: Palm Canyon Drive draws inspiration from retro matchbook covers, travel postcards, Tikki bars and Hollywood — classy yet unpretentious.

name: Comique
classification_notes: Bold hand-drawn display typeface extracted from vintage Franco-Belgian bande dessinée comic books. Intentionally limited character set — only characters that existed in original source material. Styled on Tintin/Astérix era comics.
summary: A bold display typeface extracted from vintage Franco-Belgian bande dessinée comics, preserving only the characters that appeared in original source material.
description: Comique is built from careful typographic extraction of vintage Franco-Belgian comic books — the visual tradition of Tintin and Astérix. Each glyph has been painstakingly restored from original printed sources, with the intentional limitation that only characters which existed in the source material are included. The result is an authentic display face with genuine bande dessinée character.
foundry_blurb: Comique only includes characters that existed in the source material, painstakingly restored from vintage Franco-Belgian comic pages — no frills, no filler, no invented characters.
